name: Sun
description: The star that holds it all together. The Sun contains 99.86% of the Solar System's mass — every planet, moon, asteroid and comet combined is a rounding error next to it.
facts: About 1.3 million Earths would fit inside the Sun.
Sunlight takes 8 minutes 19 seconds to reach Earth — and over 4 hours to reach Neptune.
In True Scale mode, notice how even the Sun becomes a tiny dot compared to the distances between planets.

name: Mercury
description: The smallest planet and the closest to the Sun, a cratered world of extremes: 430 °C in daylight, −180 °C at night.
facts: A single Mercury day (sunrise to sunrise) lasts 176 Earth days — longer than its year.
Sunlight reaches Mercury in just 3.2 minutes.
Despite being closest to the Sun, it is not the hottest planet — Venus is.

name: Venus
description: Earth's twin in size, wrapped in a crushing carbon-dioxide atmosphere with clouds of sulfuric acid. Its runaway greenhouse effect makes it the hottest planet.
facts: Venus spins backwards (retrograde) — the Sun rises in the west.
One rotation takes 243 Earth days, longer than its 225-day year.
Surface pressure is 92× Earth's — like being 900 m underwater.

name: Earth
description: Home. The only place in the universe where life is known to exist, orbiting the Sun at 150 million km — one Astronomical Unit, the yardstick for the whole Solar System.
facts: Sunlight you see left the Sun 8 minutes 19 seconds ago. Turn on the Sunlight travel layer to watch it happen.
In True Scale mode, Earth is about 1/100th of a pixel when the whole system is on screen.
Earth's axial tilt of 23.4° — not its distance from the Sun — causes the seasons.

name: Mars
description: The red planet, painted by iron oxide dust. Half Earth's size, with the tallest volcano (Olympus Mons, 21 km) and the deepest canyon system in the Solar System.
facts: A Mars day is 24 hours 37 minutes — eerily close to Earth's.
Light from the Sun takes 12–13 minutes to reach Mars, which is why rover commands can't be 'live'.
Its two tiny moons, Phobos and Deimos, are likely captured asteroids.

name: Jupiter
description: The giant: 2.5× the mass of all other planets combined. A ball of hydrogen and helium with no solid surface, banded by winds and storms — including the centuries-old Great Red Spot.
facts: Jupiter spins fastest of all planets: a day lasts under 10 hours.
Over 1,300 Earths would fit inside it — yet 1,000 Jupiters would fit in the Sun.
Sunlight takes about 43 minutes to reach Jupiter.

name: Saturn
description: The ringed jewel. Saturn's main rings span 280,000 km — three quarters of the Earth–Moon distance — yet are typically only about 10 metres thick.
facts: Saturn is less dense than water: it would float, given a big enough bathtub.
The rings are billions of chunks of nearly pure water ice, from dust grains to house-sized boulders.
Sunlight takes about 80 minutes to get here.

name: Uranus
description: An ice giant knocked on its side — its axis is tilted 98°, so it rolls around its orbit like a barrel. Methane in its atmosphere gives it the pale cyan colour.
facts: Each pole gets 42 years of continuous sunlight, then 42 years of darkness.
Uranus is the coldest planetary atmosphere in the Solar System: −224 °C.
It was the first planet discovered with a telescope (1781).

name: Neptune
description: The outermost planet, found in 1846 by mathematics before telescopes: its position was predicted from wobbles in Uranus's orbit. Winds here reach 2,100 km/h — the fastest known.
facts: Sunlight takes 4 hours 10 minutes to reach Neptune.
Neptune has completed just one orbit since its discovery (in 2011).
It is 30× farther from the Sun than Earth — feel that emptiness in True Scale mode.

name: Ceres
description: The largest object in the asteroid belt and the only dwarf planet in the inner Solar System. Alone it holds about a third of the belt's total mass.
facts: Bright salt deposits in Occator crater hint at briny water below the surface.
Ceres was considered a planet for half a century after its discovery in 1801.

name: Pluto
description: The most famous dwarf planet, a world of nitrogen ice plains and water-ice mountains in the Kuiper belt. Its orbit is so eccentric that it sometimes comes closer to the Sun than Neptune.
facts: A year on Pluto lasts 248 Earth years.
Pluto and its moon Charon orbit a point in space between them — a true double system.
Noon sunlight on Pluto is about as bright as dusk on Earth.

name: Haumea
description: A Kuiper-belt dwarf planet spinning so fast — one rotation every 4 hours — that it has been stretched into an egg shape.
facts: Haumea has two known moons and even a thin ring.
Its day is the shortest of any large body in the Solar System.

name: Makemake
description: A reddish Kuiper-belt dwarf planet, one of the brightest objects beyond Neptune, covered in frozen methane.
facts: Its discovery in 2005 helped trigger the debate that redefined 'planet'.
Sunlight takes over 6 hours to reach Makemake.

name: Eris
description: Slightly smaller than Pluto but more massive, Eris is the most distant dwarf planet — currently about three times farther from the Sun than Pluto.
facts: Its discovery as a 'tenth planet' in 2005 led to Pluto's reclassification a year later.
One Eris year lasts 558 Earth years.
